Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: Abrams and Van Eck (2021) frame videogame programming as a site of videogame literacies: multimodal, social, participatory meaning-making involving games, players, spectators, spaces, rules, and digital and nondigital resources. Their national survey of 1,016 U.S. librarians found that fewer than half reported videogame programming, most existing programs were recent, and adoption was shaped by first-order barriers such as funding, training, hardware, and space as well as second-order barriers such as confidence, beliefs about games, and assumptions about teen behavior.

No addition recommended. Exclude from the GBLS literature review because the article is a broad education-focused bibliometric and systematic review of gamification-based learning, not a study of game-based library services. # Khademi Zare, S., Varnaseri, A., & Bayati, N. (2024). Identifying the applications of gamification for audience attraction in public libraries. *Libri, 74*(2), 171-184. https://doi.org/10.1515/libri-2023-0123 Unique identifier: `khademizareIdentifyingApplicationsGamification2024`; Zotero item key `GA7REAB4`; attachment key `7VQXMTHM`. Khademi Zare, Varnaseri, and Bayati (2024) investigate how gamification might help public libraries attract audiences, improve participation, and make services more appealing. The article is framed around public libraries in Tehran province and the broader problem of maintaining library visibility, relevance, and use in a digital media environment. The authors argue that public libraries already have valuable resources, spaces, librarians, educational programs, and cultural services, but still need strategies that make those resources visible and engaging to diverse publics. Methodologically, the study uses a qualitative design. Data were collected through semi- structured interviews with gamification experts selected through purposive and snowball sampling. Participants needed at least two consecutive years of gamification-industry experience or comparable expert recognition. The sample grew from seven to thirteen participants to reach saturation. The authors analyzed transcripts through content/thematic analysis, following Braun and Clarke's six- stage process: familiarization, initial coding, theme searching, theme review, theme definition, and writing up. They combined interview analysis with research-literature review to identify applications of gamification for public libraries. The findings are organized into sixteen main themes and fifty-four to fifty-six subthemes. The main applications include holding events and challenges, promoting reading habits, making library interaction spaces more attractive, improving book lending, making library visits more appealing, increasing awareness of activities and programs, improving user experience, improving educational activities, increasing public-library brand loyalty, encouraging participatory and volunteer activities, nurturing talents and skills, guiding audiences toward library goals, improving book searching, developing scientific/economic activities, encouraging timely return of borrowed resources, and conducting marketing activities. The article repeatedly frames gamification as a way to combine competition, cooperation, rewards, progress tracking, feedback, personalization, dashboards, missions, ranks, badges, and social interaction. For GBLS, the strongest contribution is that the article treats audience attraction as a whole- library service problem rather than a single program type. Gamification is proposed for reading promotion, library tours, website and social-media engagement, membership processes, workshop participation, volunteer drives, peer mentoring, book searches, shelf exploration, returns, marketing, and even knowledge-production initiatives. This broad list helps the review show that game-based library service can be embedded in circulation, outreach, discovery, programming, user experience, and community participation, not only in stand-alone game nights. The article is also useful because it recognizes cultural localization. Although the authors present play as a widely shared human activity, they note that Iranian public libraries serve linguistically and culturally diverse communities and that game elements should be adapted to local cultures, values, languages, and target participants. This supports a GBLS caution: points, badges, leaderboards, and rewards cannot simply be copied from commercial systems. They need to align with audience needs, local service goals, community expectations, and equitable access. At the same time, the evidence is exploratory. The study identifies possible applications through expert interviews; it does not implement a gamified library system or measure changes in attendance, lending, reading behavior, user satisfaction, learning, retention, or volunteer participation. The authors themselves call for future comparative and controlled studies using gamified prototypes for different user groups, age segments, mechanics, point systems, narratives, and reward structures. For the GBLS review, this makes the source valuable as a design map and policy prompt, but not as proof that gamification increases public-library use. The operational implication is that large-scale public-library gamification requires infrastructure. The authors recommend a central platform, application, website, or dedicated system that can support interactions, invitations, dashboards, progress bars, rewards, marketing, and community communication. That recommendation connects the article to GBLS sustainability concerns: gameful services can become attractive and integrated, but only if libraries plan the technology, staffing, privacy, local adaptation, assessment, and maintenance needed to keep them useful. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Community, Belonging, and the Public Commons`, after the Maranchak and Maranchak (2023) paragraph. Proposed text: "Khademi Zare, Varnaseri, and Bayati (2024) extend public-library gamification from individual activities to audience attraction as a whole-library strategy. Through interviews with 13 gamification experts, they identify applications for events, reading habits, interactive spaces, lending, visits, program awareness, user experience, education, brand loyalty, volunteering, skill development, book searching, timely returns, marketing, and knowledge-production activities, showing how gameful design can be imagined across the public-library service ecology." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Start with Purpose, Audience, and Local Need`, immediately after the heading. Proposed text: "Khademi Zare et al. (2024) make local audience fit a design requirement: even though play may be broadly recognizable, game elements in public libraries should be adapted to local cultures, languages, values, service goals, and target participants rather than imported as generic points, badges, and leaderboards." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda` > `## Evidence Gaps`, after the Adetayo et al. (2023) paragraph. Proposed text: "Khademi Zare et al. (2024) add a public-library version of the same evidence gap: expert interviews can generate a rich map of possible gamification applications, but the field still needs implemented prototypes, comparison groups, age- or audience-specific testing, and measures of lending, attendance, reading, satisfaction, learning, volunteering, and retention."

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > Clubs and Recurring Play Groups`, after the Maluck (2013) paragraph. Suggested text: Alvarez (2017) adds a youth-agency rationale for recurring tabletop play in a public-library media center serving students whose schools lacked libraries. Her practitioner account describes modern tabletop games such as Splendor, Dominion, Star Realms, Zombicide, Descent, Mysterium, Pandemic, Pathfinder, Agricola, and Mansions of Madness as sequenced tools for building rule reading, strategic reassessment, cooperation, leadership, resilience, and student moderation; the evidence is observational, but it shows why game selection and facilitation should scaffold participants from approachable games toward more complex, skill-based play.

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > TTRPG and LARP Programs`, after the Maxwell (2023) paragraph. Suggested text: Anderson (2022) turns public-library D&D into a replicable teen program framework: a one-staff, three- to five-player model for ages 15-18 with an introductory character-creation session, recurring four- to five-hour play sessions, physical and remote tool lists, publicity channels, budget ranges, and informal debriefing. Its developmental- assets rationale links TTRPG play to adult relationships, safety, peer influence, creative activity, reading for pleasure, planning, interpersonal competence, conflict resolution, personal power, and self-esteem, making the format legible as youth-development programming as well as recreation.

Section and exact location: `Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy`, immediately after the section heading and before Abrams and Van Eck (2021). Suggested text: The ACRL Framework (Association of College and Research Libraries, 2016) provides the information-literacy baseline behind many game-based instruction examples. Its six frames - Authority Is Constructed and Contextual, Information Creation as a Process, Information Has Value, Research as Inquiry, Scholarship as Conversation, and Searching as Strategic Exploration - define information literacy as reflective discovery, understanding how information is produced and valued, and participating ethically in communities of learning. For GBLS, the Framework suggests that games and gamified orientations should be designed around inquiry, source authority, information creation, value, scholarly conversation, strategic searching, dispositions, metacognition, and assessment rather than only task completion.

Section and exact location: `Introduction: Why Game-Based Library Services Matter > Importance > Games have demonstrated value in education, health, culture, entertainment`, immediately after that heading. Suggested text: Barz et al. (2024) provide broad educational evidence for taking game-based learning seriously, while also showing why GBLS cannot borrow that evidence uncritically. In a meta- analysis of 35 school-based digital game-based learning studies published from 2015 to 2020, representing 7,139 pupils, DGBL produced a significant medium overall learning effect (g = .54), a significant medium cognitive effect (g = .67), a significant small affective-motivational effect (g = .32), and no significant metacognitive effect. Because the review excluded analog games, gamification, VR/AR, university/adult learners, and non-school settings, it is best used as background support for the educational promise of well-designed games, not as direct evidence for library programs. # Adetayo, A., Oladipo, Y., Adeleke, O., & Odunewu, A. (2023). Gamification of cataloguing and classification education in Nigeria library and information science schools: A technology-enhanced approach. *Journal of Education for Library and Information Science, 64*(4), 370-385. https://doi.org/10.3138/jelis-2022-0028 Unique identifier: `adetayoGamificationCataloguingClassification2023`; Zotero item key `UXSNV642`; attachment key `RJAYNGHA`. Adetayo, Oladipo, Adeleke, and Odunewu (2023) examine gamification as a possible response to difficulties in cataloguing and classification education in Nigerian library and information science schools. The article begins from a professional-education problem: cataloguing and classification are foundational to librarianship, but they are often experienced by Nigerian LIS students as technical, difficult, theory-heavy, and disconnected from practice. The authors argue that this negative perception matters because students who already see librarianship as "books and boring" may become further discouraged when core technical courses are taught through traditional methods. The article defines gamification as the use of game elements in non-game settings to create motivational experiences, not as the creation of a full game. Drawing on self-determination theory, the authors distinguish intrinsic motivation from extrinsic motivation and frame gamification as a way to increase attention, engagement, safe practice, feedback, collaboration, and progress. They also identify three broad design orientations: immersion-based game design, social-based design, and achievement/challenge-based design. For cataloguing and classification education, the most relevant parts are visible objectives, short tasks, progress feedback, manageable challenges, rewards, and peer support. Much of the article is a conceptual review rather than a field test. After surveying literature on gamification, cataloguing, classification, Nigerian LIS education, and cloud computing, the authors propose a model for technology-enhanced gamification of cataloguing and classification instruction. The model is built around five requirements: motivation, short-term tasks, a reward system, task design, and teamwork. The proposed task flow breaks cataloguing and classification into smaller stages: understanding tools, describing items, assigning subject access, producing a class mark, and placing the item correctly. A classification exercise, for example, might ask students to drag and drop books into Library of Congress classes and earn points for correct choices. Cloud computing is presented as the enabling technology because it can provide infrastructure, platforms, and software without requiring every school to upgrade local computing resources. The authors mention services such as Google Cloud Platform, Microsoft Azure, Alibaba, IBM Bluemix, and Amazon Web Services as possible deployment environments, with a smaller nod to affective computing. Their point is not that one specific platform is necessary, but that cloud delivery could allow repeated practice, continuity across sessions, scalable access to learning modules, and a shared backend for game features and learning content. For the larger GBLS project, the article is useful because it links game-based design directly to a library-specific technical curriculum. Many library-gaming sources address orientation, information literacy, reading, programs, or collections; this one asks how play can help future librarians learn the metadata and organization work that makes library collections usable. It therefore connects GBLS to cataloging, classification, professional preparation, and the infrastructure behind discovery. It also reinforces a design lesson that appears across the review: gameful systems work best when complex tasks are decomposed into smaller, meaningful challenges with feedback and opportunities to retry. The article's limits are important. The authors explicitly state that there are few or no empirical studies on gamification of cataloguing and classification in Nigerian library schools, so the model relies on gamification evidence from other educational contexts. They also warn that high-quality gamified modules have hidden costs, including design, testing, assessment, consultation with course specialists, and technology adoption. Faculty may need workshops and support before they can use gamification tools effectively. The article therefore should not be treated as evidence that a particular game improved cataloguing learning outcomes; it is better used as a proposed framework and as evidence of a professional-training and assessment gap. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Cataloging, Metadata, and Discovery`, after the Bianchini and Munini (2025) paragraph. Proposed text: "Adetayo, Oladipo, Adeleke, and Odunewu (2023) move cataloging and classification from collection description into LIS education by proposing a gamified model for teaching Nigerian students cataloguing tools, descriptive work, subject access, class marks, and shelving logic. Their model uses cloud delivery, short-term tasks, rewards, scaffolded task design, and teamwork to make technical metadata labor more practice-based and motivating, though it remains a conceptual framework rather than a tested intervention." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda` > `## Professional Practice and Training Gaps`, after the Capdarest-Arest, Opuda, and Stark (2019) paragraph. Proposed text: "Adetayo et al. (2023) broaden the training gap from practicing librarians to LIS students and faculty. Their proposed gamified cataloguing and classification model assumes that students need repeated practice and motivational scaffolding, but it also notes that faculty may need technology training, institutional support, and stakeholder coordination before such systems can be implemented in Nigerian library schools." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda` > `## Evidence Gaps`, immediately after the heading. Proposed text: "Adetayo et al. (2023) illustrate a common evidence gap in GBLS-adjacent literature: a detailed gameful model may be theoretically plausible and locally relevant, but still lack classroom testing, learning- outcome data, cost analysis, and implementation evidence. Their cataloguing and classification proposal is therefore useful as a design agenda, not yet as proof of effectiveness."

Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Curate for Inclusion, Access, and Advisory`, immediately after the Cross et al. (2023) paragraph. Suggested text: Bayeck and Bayeck (2024) add a culturally situated game-design caution for GBLS selection and facilitation. Their textual analysis of Songo and Owela argues that classic African board games should not be flattened into generic "Mancala" labels; local names, materials, spaces, directions of play, seeds, holes, cattle metaphors, forest and semiarid landscapes, and community philosophies all shape what the games mean and how players connect them to ancestry, memory, and place. The article is adjacent rather than library evidence, but it supports culturally relevant game advisory: libraries should describe, select, teach, and facilitate culturally specific tabletop games with attention to community knowledge and learner context rather than treating them as interchangeable mechanics.

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > Programs Inside Game Worlds and Virtual Platforms`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: Bell and Trueman's edited volume (2008), represented here through its table of contents and a contemporary book review, marks an early library moment in Second Life and other multi-user virtual environments. The volume maps virtual branch libraries, avatar-based reference, in-world collections, Teen Second Life, HealthInfo Island, historical simulations, speculative-fiction and mystery programming, distance-learning support, and MLIS instruction, showing that game-like virtual worlds were treated as service sites rather than only promotional novelties. Its limits are also instructive for later GBLS: virtual platforms required staff learning, institutional support, etiquette management, hardware capacity, and arguments against the perception that work inside a virtual world was "only a game."

Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Start with Purpose, Audience, and Local Need`, after the Khademi Zare et al. (2024) paragraph. Suggested text: Bishoff, Farrell, and Neeser (2015) turn local fit into a cautionary academic-library case. At the University of Minnesota - Twin Cities, two well-promoted Wii-based finals-week game events drew little sustained participation, and a first-year survey showed only modest interest in gaming events or video game checkout; the authors therefore warn against inferring demand from general gamer demographics or from programs that worked at smaller, residential, or public-library sites. Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Build Partnerships and Community Pipelines`, after the Monnier and Winters (2022) paragraph. Suggested text: Bishoff et al. (2015) also show how partnership can become the service model rather than a supplement to a collection or event. After the weak pilot events, the University of Minnesota Libraries worked with student gaming groups, the Society for Physics Students, local community partners, Co-Op Talk, game- development students, and interdisciplinary researchers, using gaming technologies and librarian facilitation to support networking, game creation, education, and community outreach.

Section and exact location: `Scope, Definition, and Review Approach > Source Base`, after the Mattson (2021) paragraph. Suggested text: Blummer and Kenton (2019) provide adjacent source-base context by reviewing 174 papers on academic-library outreach from 2008 to January 2019. Gaming appears as one small specialized-program subtheme, alongside instruction, roaming services, orientations, exhibits, makerspaces, and collections; the review is too broad to evidence GBLS outcomes, but it shows that video-game events, information-literacy games, and library-orientation games were being treated as academic-library outreach tools within wider patterns of relevance, collaboration, cultural artifacts, flexibility, marketing, management, and uneven assessment.

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Community, Belonging, and the Public Commons`, after the Vanden Elzen and Roush (2013) paragraph. Suggested text: Bremer (2017) gives a small academic-public library partnership example of tabletop games as community bridge. By moving a University of Minnesota Morris game program to the Morris Public Library, the partners used a more accessible civic venue, shared game collections and staff expertise, food, broad publicity, and simple family-friendly board and card games to bring together college students, adults, families, and children, while also identifying teens as a still-unreached audience. Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Build Partnerships and Community Pipelines`, after the Vanden Elzen and Roush (2013) paragraph. Suggested text: Bremer (2017) also shows that GBLS partnerships can reduce place-based barriers. The academic library had games and staff experience, while the public library had a central community location and local audience; together they could host a tabletop Game Day neither library could have delivered as effectively alone, using Friends-group support, shared publicity, rule facilitation, and post-event reflection to plan the next iteration.

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Emerging Area: Wellness, Mental Health, and Care`, immediately after the Lee et al. (2024) paragraph. Suggested text: Brennan (2023) offers a small school-library practitioner example of games as part of a wellbeing ecology rather than a standalone program. In redeveloping Stonyhurst College's library from a primarily silent study space into a more welcoming third place, Brennan introduced break-time activities including chess, computer games, and film screenings alongside environmental changes, Mental Health First Aid training, and a Reading Well collection. The article is illustrative rather than evaluative, but it shows how low-barrier play can support friendship, conversation, relaxation, and belonging within a broader student-centered mental-health service model.

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy`, after the Smith and Baker (2011) paragraph. Suggested text: Broussard (2014) links academic-library instruction games directly to formative assessment. Using *Secret Agents in the Library* and *Goblin Threat* as examples, she argues that games can make one-shot information literacy instruction more responsive by embedding feedback, scaffolding, safe failure, social learning, self-assessment, and debriefing into play, so evidence of learning appears while librarians can still correct misconceptions. Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Evaluate Outcomes Beyond Attendance`, after the Brown and Kasper (2013) paragraph. Suggested text: Broussard (2014) also reframes assessment itself as a design problem: games should connect learning objectives to activities early, provide specific and immediate feedback, and use wraparound reflection such as clicker review or discussion to help students transfer game experience into later research behavior. This supports GBLS assessment approaches that look for formative evidence during play, not only post-program satisfaction.

Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Evaluate Outcomes Beyond Attendance`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: Brown and Kasper (2013) make the assessment problem explicit through ninety hours of participant observation in a public- library video game club. Their case shows that attendance and enjoyment miss much of what game clubs may do: staff modeled information seeking, enforced rules, mentored youth, and connected play to reading, technology, local history, behavior, academic standing, library cards, book checkouts, and social belonging. They recommend richer measures of reading motivation, library anxiety, information literacy, behavior, skill development, and reward-based progress so libraries can evaluate whether gaming programs advance learning and community goals rather than merely drawing participants.

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Collections, Circulation, and Access`, after the Lee, Clarke, Cho, and Windleharth (2017) paragraph. Suggested text: Buchanan and Vanden Elzen (2012) offer an early practitioner framework for adding video games to library collections and programs through the familiar service values of access, motivation, and guidance. Their argument treats games as visual, interactive simulations that require critical literacy and can support discovery, advisory, civic discussion, cross-media connections, circulating collections, game labs, kiosks, curriculum collections, and programming for patrons ranging from teens to educators and game scholars.

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > Circulating and In-Library Collections`, after the Robson, Phillips, and Guerrero (2018) paragraph. Suggested text: Buller (2017) extends circulating game collections from software and tabletop materials to console hardware. At the University of Denver, console lending supported an interdisciplinary game design curriculum by giving students access to multiple platforms and generations, portable screens, controllers, holds, renewals, component checklists, serial-number verification, profile logout procedures, and privacy protections; the case shows that game collections often require lending the access infrastructure around games, not only the games themselves. Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Research, Scholarship, and Creative Production`, after the Wood and Carter (2018) paragraph. Suggested text: Buller (2017) also links game hardware lending to academic creative production. Because students in game design courses needed to compare how games used different console affordances, the library treated PlayStation, Xbox, and Nintendo systems as mission-aligned curricular resources analogous to other hardware needed to access specialized media.

Section and exact location: `Introduction: Why Game-Based Library Services Matter > The role of Libraries is everchanging`, replacing the existing Burek Pierce (2016) paragraph. Suggested text: Today's game-based and multimedia library programs are not new; they continue a long-standing struggle to balance imagination and instruction, play and professionalism in library service. Burek Pierce (2016) shows that between 1876 and 1925, children's librarians used toys, games, pictures, maps, puzzles, scrapbook-making, lantern slides, and realia as child-centered service media informed by the child study and kindergarten movements. These resources did not displace books; they linked tactile, visual, social, and creative learning to reading, memory, curiosity, peer interaction, and a welcoming children's room. The later retreat from playful youth services came as library leaders sought efficiency, uniformity, dignity, and alignment with adult departments, treating the "experimental temptation" of nonbook media as a threat to professional seriousness. For GBLS, this history reframes games as a recurring public-library strategy rather than a digital-age novelty. # Giles, K. (2015). No budget, no experience, no problem: Creating a library orientation game for freshman engineering majors. *The Journal of Academic Librarianship, 41*(2), 170-177. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.acalib.2014.12.005 Unique identifier: `gilesNoBudgetNo2015`; Zotero item key `EWFRJ3U8`; attachment key `2UCCMHZJ`. Giles (2015) describes *Mystery at the Library*, a simple and inexpensive library-orientation game created for first-year engineering majors at James Madison University's Rose Library. The local problem was specific but common: engineering freshmen needed to know Rose Library's science, technology, and health collections, subject librarian offices, spaces, reserve materials, catalog, call-number system, and RefWorks, but the engineering curriculum offered little space for lower-level library instruction. The librarian wanted an out-of-class activity that would introduce the building, provide a shared experience before a later in-class session, and fit engineering students' preference for hands-on exploration. The game adapted scavenger-hunt and alternate-reality-game ideas into a library mystery. Students were told that a fictional notebook belonging to Nikola Tesla had been stolen from a Rose Library display case. Suspect profiles, instructions, and backstory were distributed through the course website. Players began by finding the first clue on reserve, then followed photocopied citations, highlighted references, call numbers, and red-folder clues through the catalog, stacks, reference section, bound journals, and a thief's RefWorks account. The mystery structure replaced a worksheet with a narrative role: students were not simply answering a list of questions, but searching the library and online to identify the thief. The design constraints are one of the article's strongest contributions. There was no budget for special materials, programming, video production, or actors, and the librarian had no previous game-design experience. The game therefore used existing library resources, class-web materials, paper clues, and red pocket folders costing less than a dollar each. It also had to be quiet, self- directed, and minimally disruptive to normal library operations. Desk staff tested the game before students played and later provided feedback that shaped revisions. Giles is especially useful for GBLS because she reports iterative design changes. Early versions included a detective character, a blog and Gmail account, coded-message puzzles, and email interactions intended to add atmosphere. Player confusion and staff burden showed that these features were not worth keeping if they did not teach library skills. The game was simplified so that the challenge remained tied to learning objectives. A difficult task involving *The Dictionary of Energy* was retained because it revealed a real library-use problem: students did not understand that "Rose Lib-Reference" meant the first- floor reference section. The library responded by adding signage. Later revisions added a thief's "to do" list to clarify the structure and expand the game into the periodicals area. The 2012 assessment compared two engineering course sections that played the game with a third control section that did not. Thirty-four of 44 students in the experimental group and 22 of 24 in the control group completed an online survey before any of the sections received in-class instruction. The control group reported a preference for learning by trying things and figuring them out. The experimental group reported greater familiarity with all listed tasks: getting reserve items, looking up books in the catalog, finding books on shelves, finding periodicals, finding the reference section, and logging in to RefWorks. The largest difference was RefWorks familiarity. Player response was favorable: 88% agreed that they learned about the library, 74% felt more comfortable using it, 71% felt more comfortable asking for help, 62% had fun, and 79% would recommend the activity for future classes. For the GBLS project, Giles contributes a pragmatic orientation model. A low-cost library mystery can scaffold later instruction, make physical exploration meaningful, create reasons to interact with staff, reveal confusing service points, and turn existing collections into game materials. The case also cautions that atmosphere should not outweigh purpose. Narrative is useful when it structures action and creates continuity for later teaching, such as discussions of ethical information use, source evaluation, and search-term construction; extra puzzles or characters are distractions when they increase confusion without serving learning goals. The limitations are also clear: the study measures self-reported familiarity, not objective learning or long-term transfer, and it focuses on one engineering cohort at one institution. Still, it is a strong GBLS implementation source for libraries with limited money, staff, or game-design expertise. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game- Based Library Service` > `## Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy`, after the Smith and Baker (2011) paragraph. Proposed text: "Giles (2015) provides a closely related freshman- engineering orientation case through *Mystery at the Library*, a no-budget paper-and-web mystery about a stolen Nikola Tesla notebook. The game used reserve materials, catalog searching, call numbers, reference and periodicals locations, red-folder clues, and a RefWorks account to move students through Rose Library; a small self-assessment comparison found higher reported familiarity with library tasks among players than nonplayers, while revisions showed that simple narrative and objective-aligned puzzles worked better than atmospheric complexity." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Design for Reflection and Transfer`, after the Guo and Goh (2016) paragraph. Proposed text: "Giles (2015) reinforces this design principle from a low-resource angle: keep the narrative simple, remove puzzles or characters that do not teach the service goal, retain challenges that reveal genuine library-use barriers, and use the game story afterward to bridge into source evaluation, ethical information use, search terms, and database limiters."

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Instruction, Orientation, and Information Literacy`, immediately after Seay's "A Liminal Response to Watching Paint Dry" paragraph. Suggested text: Burgess and Wallace (2021) shift game-based instruction from library orientation to reference education. Using concept analysis, they argue that reference workers need clinical intuition and metacognition to adapt interviews, resource selection, and search construction in real time, and that investigative video games can supplement MLIS reference teaching by letting students practice abductive, deductive, and inductive reasoning through rule testing and immediate feedback in games such as *Her Story*, *Return of the Obra Dinn*, and *Subserial Network*. The article is conceptual rather than outcome evidence, but it gives GBLS a vocabulary for using commercial games as adjacent simulations for professional judgment, especially in online or asynchronous LIS courses. # Miller, R. E. (2019). Libraries may teach some skills through mobile application games. *Evidence Based Library and Information Practice, 14*(3), 150-152. https://doi.org/10.18438/eblip29587 Unique identifier: `millerLibrariesMayTeach2019`; Zotero item key `TWRZ3LTD`. Miller (2019) is an evidence summary and critical appraisal of Kaneko, Saito, Nohara, Kudo, and Yamada's (2018) study of "Library Adventures: Unveil the Hidden Mysteries!," a mobile application game for university library instruction at Kyushu University in Fukuoka, Japan. The reviewed study compared a game-based learning environment with a web-based tutorial for first- year undergraduate students. Thirty-six students began the experiment, 32 completed it, and participants were divided into experimental and control groups. Both groups received instruction on local library procedures, including library hours, collection arrangement, and methods for locating books and articles. The control group used a web tutorial, while the experimental group used a mobile game that required students to move through the physical library and complete location-based tasks. The design included a pretest, learning experience, post-test, motivation questionnaire, and a one-month follow-up with declarative knowledge and behavioral tests. The evidence summary is especially useful because it distinguishes several kinds of outcomes that are often blurred in library game reports. Miller reports that the game and tutorial groups gained similar levels of declarative knowledge, and that both groups lost some knowledge after one month. The mobile-game group, however, performed better on some embodied library tasks, such as borrowing a journal and locating a newspaper article, while tutorial users completed study-room reservation more quickly. The strongest reported difference was motivational: students in the game condition scored higher on attention, relevance, and satisfaction in the Instructional Materials Motivation Survey, which draws on Keller's ARCS model of Attention, Relevance, Confidence, and Satisfaction. Confidence did not differ between groups. Miller's commentary emphasizes why the Kaneko et al. study matters for library instruction: it directly compares a game and a tutorial rather than only describing a single game intervention, and the game requires physical movement through the library rather than treating mobile delivery as a purely screen-based experience. In GBLS terms, "Library Adventures" is not simply a digital object; it is a hybrid service format connecting mobile prompts, physical navigation, local procedures, and task performance. The article therefore supports a view of game- based library instruction as situated practice. A game may not produce superior quiz scores, but it may better support learners in connecting procedural knowledge to embodied library use. At the same time, Miller's appraisal cautions against overclaiming. The original study's recruitment, consent, inclusion criteria, and reporting are not fully described, and the relationship between game-stage analytics, pre/post-test outcomes, and motivation measures remains underdeveloped. For the larger GBLS project, this makes the source valuable less as proof that mobile games "work" and more as a compact example of what stronger GBLS evaluation might include: comparison groups, delayed follow- up, behavioral tasks, validated motivation instruments, and attention to where players succeed or struggle inside the game. It also reinforces that assessment should separate knowledge retention, task performance, motivation, confidence, and transfer rather than collapsing them into attendance or enjoyment. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Evaluate Outcomes Beyond Attendance`, after the Broussard (2014) paragraph. Proposed text: "Miller's (2019) evidence summary of Kaneko et al.'s mobile *Library Adventure* study reinforces this assessment lesson. The reviewed experiment found similar declarative-knowledge gains for game and tutorial users, mixed behavioral performance after one month, and stronger game-group motivation on attention, relevance, and satisfaction; for GBLS, the value lies in separating motivation, retention, task performance, confidence, and transfer instead of treating game enjoyment as an outcome by itself."

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Cataloging, Metadata, and Discovery`, immediately after the Lee, Cho, Fox, and Perti (2013) paragraph. She finds that emerging game schemas converge on medium-specific elements that RDA treats awkwardly or does not make core: platform distinct from edition and system requirements, equal attention to developer and publisher, granular media and platform vocabularies, release/update/version/additional-content data, and multifaceted genre split across narrative, visual, and gameplay facets. For GBLS, her analysis shows that game cataloging can remain crosswalkable to RDA while still needing enhanced description for scholarly access, player discovery, and legacy software preservation. # Nance, M. K. (2022). Playing for keeps: How academic libraries are prioritizing student mental health and well-being through play. *Journal of Play in Adulthood, 4*(2), 163-176. Unique identifier: `nancePlayingKeepsHow2022`; Zotero item key `BUW225C6`; attachment key `RSWIGWEQ`. Nance (2022) argues that academic libraries have become active contributors to student mental health and well-being by providing play-based materials, spaces, and programs. The article is a narrative synthesis and professional rationale rather than a new empirical evaluation, but it is directly relevant to Game Based Library Services because it reframes play as part of holistic academic support. Nance begins from the claim that higher education has historically separated academic success from mental health support, assigning the former to teaching faculty, libraries, and academic-support units and the latter to counseling, health, residential life, and student life. Rising levels of student anxiety, depression, and stress make that separation less tenable. Because academic libraries are centrally located, open for long hours, already trusted as student gathering spaces, and tied to reliable information, they are positioned to normalize low-barrier wellness support. The article's core move is to connect adult playfulness to student coping, academic success, and healthy breaks. Nance summarizes research suggesting that play and playfulness in adulthood can increase engagement, creativity, mindfulness, innovation, social skills, coping, and cognitive performance while decreasing boredom, stress, tension, depression, aggression, and anxiety. She also links student mental health to academic performance and retention, arguing that libraries' academic mission now requires attention to emotional wellness. Play-based library services are not substitutes for counseling or therapy, but their accessibility and nonclinical setting may reduce stigma, support self-reflection, and help students practice balance, mindfulness, and stress relief. Nance identifies several practical service forms. First, academic libraries can support micro-breaks: short interruptions from work or study that help restore focus, reduce fatigue, and maintain engagement. Coloring pages, Legos, Play- Doh, kinetic sand, puzzles, video games, and similar materials give students a structured way to step away from intensive study while keeping their attention grounded in an unrelated task. Second, coloring initiatives are presented as inexpensive, customizable, social, and meditative activities. Adult coloring books, mandalas, geometric images, and coloring stations offer enough structure to avoid anxiety about creating from scratch while still allowing choice, control, and mindfulness. Third, Nance discusses Legos and building materials as kinetic resources for stress relief, creative thinking, collaboration, and feelings of accomplishment. She notes examples such as a Lego playing station at Rutgers' Art Library, Princeton's Build and Play section in a library makerspace, and Columbia's in-library-use Lego build kits. These materials matter because they create shared, tactile spaces where students can make something, focus away from coursework, and return to study with a different emotional state. Fourth, Nance argues that video games can support psychological and physiological stress relief by offering immersion, distraction, autonomy, competence, relatedness, and social play. Circulating consoles, providing designated gaming spaces, and offering casual gameplay can reach students who may not be drawn to more conventional wellness activities. For the GBLS project, the article is useful because it widens the definition of game-based library services beyond instruction, collections, or entertainment. It treats games and playful materials as care infrastructure. A coloring station, Lego kit, video game console, therapy-animal event, puzzle table, scavenger hunt, escape room, or library mini-golf program can become part of a broader wellness ecology when it gives students permission to take breaks, socialize, regulate stress, and choose healthier coping practices. This is especially important for academic libraries, where prolonged study, finals culture, and stigma around breaks can produce an unhealthy model of dedication. The limitations are important. Nance does not evaluate a particular library intervention or provide outcome data from students using play-based wellness services. Some supporting evidence comes from broader play, art therapy, adult playfulness, counseling, and gaming research rather than from library-specific assessment. Still, the article gives the literature review a needed bridge between wellness research and concrete academic-library practice. It supports a GBLS agenda in which libraries evaluate not only attendance and satisfaction, but also stress relief, belonging, healthy coping, access to wellness resources, normalization of breaks, and the relationship between playful library spaces and student academic persistence. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Emerging Area: Wellness, Mental Health, and Care`, after the Brennan (2023) paragraph. Proposed text: "Nance (2022) extends the wellness thread to academic libraries by framing play as holistic student-support infrastructure. Coloring stations, Legos, Play-Doh, kinetic sand, puzzles, video games, therapy-animal events, scavenger hunts, escape rooms, and library mini-golf can function as low-barrier micro-breaks that normalize taking a break, reduce stigma around help-seeking, support mindfulness and healthy coping, and connect mental well-being to academic persistence." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Programming Formats and Service Models` > `## Circulating and In-Library Collections`, after the Slobuski, Robson, and Bentley (2017) paragraph. Proposed text: "Nance (2022) also shows that circulating and in-library collections can be wellness infrastructure, not only recreational inventory. Academic libraries' circulating consoles, Lego build kits, coloring supplies, puzzles, Play-Doh, and kinetic materials give students flexible access to play-based breaks, social connection, and stress relief in the same spaces where they study."
No suggested addition. This is a non-library secondary-school physics DGBL study and should remain in `Explicitly Excluded Literature` after summary completion.

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > Escape Rooms and Breakout Boxes`, immediately after the `## Escape Rooms and Breakout Boxes` heading. Suggested text: Cho et al. In 17 sessions across five Washington public-library systems, *The Euphorigen Investigation* used collaborative play, a fictional misinformation scenario, a designed deepfake failure, and librarian-led debriefing to increase awareness of misinformation tactics, prompt reflection on confirmation bias and vulnerability, and encourage more cautious attitudes toward sharing information online.

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Programs Inside Game Worlds and Virtual Platforms`, immediately after the Bell and Trueman (2008) paragraph. Suggested text: Chow et al. (2012) provide empirical grounding for this early Second Life moment through a mixed- method usability and needs assessment involving Olathe Public Library, its Second Life branch, other virtual librarians, and 366 total participants. They found a weak match between traditional public- library users and virtual users: local patrons were often unaware of or only modestly interested in Second Life until shown examples, while virtual library visitors were frequently first-time explorers with diffuse information needs who rarely used static resources or spoke with librarians. The study suggests that virtual-world library services work best when they emphasize community, events, exhibits, in-world help, classes, social connection, and experiences not possible in physical branches, while also warning that volunteer labor, limited staff time, platform learning curves, promotion gaps, and the perception that Second Life is "just a game" make sustainability difficult. # Capdarest-Arest, N., Opuda, E., & Stark, R. K. (2019). "Game on!" Teaching gamification principles for library instruction to health sciences information professionals using interactive, low-tech activities and design-thinking modalities. *Journal of the Medical Library Association, 107*(4), 566-571. https://doi.org/10.5195/jmla.2019.636 Unique identifier: `capdarest- arestGameTeachingGamification2019`; Zotero item key `7XLJGKK4`; attachment key `DEEDAEAC`. Capdarest-Arest, Opuda, and Stark (2019) report on "Design, Play, Learn," a ninety-minute special content session at the 2018 Medical Library Association annual meeting designed to teach health sciences information professionals how to create low-tech games for library instruction. The article is not an evaluation of student learning from a library game; instead, it is professional- development evidence about how librarians can learn gamification principles, design-thinking methods, and rapid prototyping practices. This distinction matters for the GBLS review because staff confidence, training, and design capacity are recurring implementation barriers. The article frames low-tech gamification as an accessible alternative to high-tech games. High-tech games may require programming skills, software, funding, devices, and time that many instruction librarians and learners do not have. Low-tech games, including analog card, board, and classroom games, can be prototyped quickly, played face to face, and tested with ordinary materials. The authors pair this low-resource orientation with design thinking: empathize, define, ideate, prototype, and test. For library instruction, that process means identifying recurring instructional problems, brainstorming game-based solutions, building prototypes, and testing whether the game helps address the original teaching need. The session itself combined flipped preparation, short didactic instruction, active learning, and team-based making. Participants received suggested readings, a short presentation on gamification, a behavior agreement, a shared Google Drive folder, and a sample low-tech card game for database-search instruction. The sample game asked health sciences students to practice creating database search strings using Boolean operator cards and health-specific terminology cards. During the session, attendees worked in self-selected groups of two to eight with pens, markers, colored paper, index cards, dice, rubber bands, scissors, paper clips, and other ordinary supplies. They could adapt the sample game or design their own, write rules, create game pieces, take photos, and upload materials to a shared folder for reuse under a Creative Commons model. The reported assessment is modest but useful. Forty-three participants completed the pre-session survey and 35 completed the post-session survey, although the authors could not calculate a return rate because attendees could enter and leave freely. Before the session, 12 of 43 respondents (28%) reported feeling confident using gamification in a library instruction setting; after the session, 29 of 35 respondents (83%) did. The percentage planning to use what they learned in future instruction rose from 77% to 94%, and the percentage planning to use the sample game or a game built during the session rose from 46% to 86%. Qualitative feedback suggested that participants found the session helpful, wanted more time to play and debrief, and made connections for future collaboration. For the larger GBLS project, the article helps define facilitation and professional preparation as infrastructure. Libraries cannot scale game-based instruction simply by collecting examples of games; staff need ways to practice game design, test rules, prototype with constraints, assess learning, and adapt ideas to local classrooms. The article's emphasis on low-tech materials is especially important for equity and sustainability. It shows that professional development can lower the threshold for experimentation by making game design tactile, collaborative, iterative, and achievable inside a short workshop. The article's limits are clear. The surveys measure self- reported confidence and intention, not whether participants later implemented games or whether those games improved student learning. The session was informal, attendance was fluid, and the sample was limited to MLA conference attendees. Still, the case is valuable because it documents a replicable training format for librarians: short reading preparation, brief conceptual framing, shared materials, a sample game, hands-on group prototyping, informal testing, shared repositories, and follow-up plans. For GBLS, this is evidence that training design itself can be game-like, low-cost, and practice-oriented. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda` > `## Professional Practice and Training Gaps`, after the Elkins and Hollister (2020) paragraph. Proposed text: "Capdarest-Arest, Opuda, and Stark (2019) make professional preparation more concrete through a ninety-minute MLA workshop that taught health sciences librarians low-tech game design and design-thinking methods. Participants used ordinary materials to prototype database-instruction games, and self-reported confidence using gamification rose from 28% before the session to 83% after it; the study is short-term and self-reported, but it shows that GBLS training can focus on rapid prototyping, rule testing, shared repositories, and low-cost instructional design rather than only on finished game examples." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Treat Facilitation as Core Infrastructure`, immediately after the heading. Proposed text: "Capdarest-Arest et al. (2019) also show that facilitation capacity can be taught through the same playful methods GBLS uses with patrons. Their low-tech workshop positioned librarians as designers and testers, giving them practice with empathizing, defining instructional problems, ideating, prototyping, testing, and adapting games with accessible materials."

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > Circulating and In-Library Collections`, immediately after the Buller (2017) paragraph. Suggested text: Cross, Mould, and Smith (2015) broaden the academic-library collection model through Carleton University Library's mix of board games, console games, Steam-based PC games, and a dedicated gaming lab. Their case shows why GBLS collections must account for format-specific access models: board games expose mechanics for design analysis, console games circulate easily but require platform and replacement decisions, PC/indie games may require lab access because DRM and non-transferable licenses break ordinary lending, and mobile games/MMOs may not fit library access at all. The article also makes operations visible, including challenged-materials policies, staff resistance, accessories, DLC, digital subscriptions, preservation, MARC/RDA cataloging, browsable shelving, replacement fees, room booking, acceptable-use rules, computing support, and senior-management backing.

Section and exact location: `Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services > Curate for Inclusion, Access, and Advisory`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: Cross et al. (2023) provide adjacent audience data for tabletop GBLS through a survey and open dataset of 1,603 board gamers' demographics, preferences, and motivations. The sample was mostly men, white, highly educated, and active in the hobby, but it also included substantial variation and more than one- quarter of respondents reporting a mental health or neurodevelopmental condition; respondents generally preferred in-person 3-4 player games lasting 30-60 minutes, cards and boards, competitive all-vs-all or cooperative styles, Euro/hybrid designs, and were motivated most by social interaction and strategising rather than competition. Because the study samples hobby board gamers rather than library patrons, it should not define library demand, but it can help libraries choose tabletop formats with attention to inclusion, accessibility, group size, session length, social goals, and population-specific preferences.

Section and exact location: `Programming Formats and Service Models > VR, AR, and Immersive Technology`, immediately after the Lee, King, Dahya, and Lee (2020) paragraph. Suggested text: Dahya et al. (2019) provide the broader report behind that Washington public-library VR project, adding patron-side and equity evidence from 185 surveys, 39 patron interviews, site observations, and approximately 400 VR users. The report shows that patrons overwhelmingly enjoyed VR and often learned how to use the technology, but it also complicates claims about empathy, content learning, and equity: participation skewed toward gamers, boys, men, and White patrons except where local patron demographics differed, and meaningful learning required clearer outcomes and wraparound pedagogy. Its implementation guidance emphasizes lead time, staff practice, waivers and age restrictions, public versus private setup, safety, themed outreach, adjacent activities, and movement from VR consumption toward creation.

Section and exact location: `Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service > Key Game Studies Debates Relevant to Libraries`, after the Wang and Huang (2023) paragraph and before `### Digital vs Analog Game Debate`. Suggested text: Danilovic and de Voogt (2021) add a useful conceptual caution for abstract tabletop GBLS. Their cross-ludic theory distinguishes the reliable formal systems of abstract board games from the variable local meanings, materials, names, and cultural practices attached to them; for libraries, this means abstract games may be portable and low-material without being culturally blank, so facilitation and advisory should attend both to rules and to community- specific interpretation.

Section and exact location: `Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service > Cataloging, Metadata, and Discovery`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: de Groat (2015) provides the historical foundation for game cataloging as a GBLS infrastructure problem. Her account shows that U.S. video game cataloging moved through shifting AACR2, MARC, and RDA treatments of machine-readable data files, computer files, electronic resources, moving images, carriers, system requirements, and platform notes, while subject, genre, and preferred-title practices remained tied to book-centered Library of Congress policies that never fully accounted for games as complex interactive works. Section and exact location: `Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda > Infrastructure and Metadata Gaps`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: de Groat (2015) also clarifies why metadata gaps persist: platform information is often trapped in notes or improvised fields, game genre headings are inconsistent or topical rather than true genre/form access points, "computer games" and "video games" are not cleanly distinguished, franchise and title authority work lacks clear guidance, and the Library of Congress no longer catalogs games within the workflow that generates the policies most U.S. libraries follow. These gaps limit discovery, advisory, preservation, and scholarly use of game collections.

No suggested addition. Explicitly exclude from the GBLS literature review because this editorial is a broad academic-library public-services framing piece rather than a study or substantive discussion of game-based library services.

Section and exact location: `Games, Gamification, and Play`, immediately after Huizinga (1950) and before Seay (2018). Suggested text: Deterding, Dixon, Khaled, and Nacke (2011) provide the canonical definition of gamification as the use of game design elements in non-game contexts. Their University of Idaho case moved a roughly 110-game LibGuide into a CollectionBuilder site by scraping unstructured LibGuide pages with OpenRefine, converting them to a structured Google Sheet, renaming book-oriented fields such as author to designer, adding game-specific fields for awards, player count, age, play time, mechanics, themes, images, and thumbnails, and exposing browsing through cards, word clouds, category pages, and multi-field search. Section and exact location: `Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda > Professional Practice and Training Gaps`, immediately after the heading. Suggested text: Guo and Lan (2023) provide a more empirical virtual-platform example through two public-library EFL reading projects with 38 children in Taiwan. After a picture-storybook, Q&A, and word-game project improved reading scores but left motivation and anxiety concerns, the revised project added OIV, a desktop 3D virtual-world authoring tool, so learners could plan stories on posters and then build multimodal story worlds with text, objects, spatial cues, and interactive questions. The PB+VR group improved reading, showed higher motivation and lower anxiety, and produced richer multimodal artifacts, suggesting that library virtual-world programs work best when they connect immersive creation to literacy goals, planning, collaboration, feedback, and artifact sharing rather than treating VR as novelty.

Section and exact location: `Conceptual Terrain: Games, Play, and Library Service > Games, Gamification, and Play`, immediately after the section heading and before Seay (2018). Suggested text: Huizinga (1950) supplies a classic conceptual baseline for treating play as a cultural form rather than mere recreation. His account defines play as voluntary, bounded in time and space, rule- governed, absorbing, and set apart from ordinary life, while also generating order, tension, social groupings, and a durable play-community; for GBLS, the point is not to adopt Huizinga wholesale, but to recognize that library games work by creating temporary civic spaces with accepted rules, meaningful stakes, and communities that may outlast the event.

(2025). ClaG: A classification system for plays and games in toy libraries and libraries. *JLIS.it, 16*(1), 73-89. https://doi.org/10.36253/jlis.it-624 Unique identifier: `bianchiniClaGClassificationSystem2025`; Zotero item key `ZTU5FAVS`; attachment key `A9F7WVQD`. Bianchini and Munini (2025) present ClaG, a classification system for plays and games developed for the Archivio Italiano del Gioco (AIG), the Italian Games Archive in Udine. The article begins from a practical collection problem. AIG holds about 2,800 board games and more than 700 publications, and its game collection needs physical organization, cataloging, classification, and a usable arrangement for players, researchers, educators, and library staff. Existing local tags were not enough because they lacked notation, definitions, guidance, and a systematic structure suitable for organizing a physical collection. The central claim is that game classification should be designed from the player's perspective. Bianchini and Munini adapt Ranganathan's account of bibliographic classification by asking how games can be arranged in a helpful sequence, replaced correctly after use, and placed meaningfully among already-held games. For a game library or a library with a game collection, classification should help staff and users select, research, identify, and deliver a game or set of games to a player or group of players. This makes the article directly relevant to GBLS infrastructure: classification is not just back-office order, but a service tool for matching games to local use. Methodologically, ClaG was built through both top-down and bottom-up work. The top-down component drew on play and game literature, including Huizinga, Caillois, Salen and Zimmerman, Adams, game-design manuals, encyclopedias, dictionaries, and game scholarship. The bottom-up component involved recursively applying and revising candidate concepts while classifying more than 200 games, mainly table and board games. Game experts and volunteers tested preliminary versions, and the system was presented in two seminars where participants suggested revisions. ClaG begins by distinguishing play, game, and toy. Play is treated broadly as a free, spontaneous activity with rules, boundaries, uncertain outcome, and fictitious character. Game is treated as a form of play involving rules, artificial conflict, arbitrary goals, and quantifiable outcomes. Toy is treated not as an object in itself but as material for play, especially mimicry play. From these definitions, the classification develops several major facets. The first is space, based on the player's position relative to the play- ground: play without a precisely defined space, real-scale play-ground, reduced-scale play-ground, and virtual play-ground. The second is materials, including games without materials, ball games, card games, dice games, tile games, paper-and-pencil games, and toys. The third facet is setting: thematic games, non-thematic games, and simulation games. This matters because setting can drive educational selection, advisory, and curricular use. The fourth is game outcome, including solitaire, single winner, single winning team, cooperative, semi-cooperative, single loser, and no winner. The fifth is genre, built from Caillois's agon, alea, mimicry, and vertigo but elaborated for classification by focusing on player attitude and required skills. Agonistic games, for example, can be subdivided by language, storytelling, logic, mathematics, deduction, abduction, dexterity, strength, balance, coordination, speed, endurance, and other skill types. After expert feedback, a sixth facet, age, was added because librarians and toy-library practitioners considered suggested player age important for selection. For the larger GBLS project, Bianchini and Munini are important because they connect game metadata, physical arrangement, advisory, and access. Many earlier metadata sources focus on video games, digital platforms, narrative description, or preservation artifacts. ClaG instead offers a classification approach for toy libraries and libraries with analog game collections, especially board and tabletop games. Its facets correspond to questions patrons and facilitators actually ask: Where can this be played? What materials are needed? Does it have a theme? How many players can win? Is it cooperative, competitive, or solitary? What skills or play attitudes does it require? What ages is it suitable for? These are service questions as much as cataloging questions. The source should also be read with its limits in mind. The published article gives an overview rather than the full tables, and the current system is strongest for board games. Traditional children's games, sports, mimicry games, role-playing games, and video games are acknowledged as needing fuller development. Evaluation was preliminary, based on expert testing and seminar feedback rather than broad field deployment. Even so, the article gives the review a useful contemporary example of game classification as practical GBLS infrastructure and points toward future collaborative development through a Wikibase Cloud instance. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Cataloging, Metadata, and Discovery`, after the Post et al. (2023) paragraph. Proposed text: "Bianchini and Munini (2025) extend game description from metadata records to shelf classification and player-facing selection through ClaG, a classification system for plays and games developed for the Italian Games Archive. Built through play/game theory, Ranganathanian classification principles, expert testing, and iterative classification of more than 200 mostly tabletop games, ClaG organizes games by space, materials, setting, game outcome, genre, and age so toy libraries and libraries with game collections can arrange, identify, research, and deliver games according to how patrons actually choose and use them." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Gaps in the Literature and Future Research Agenda` > `## Infrastructure and Metadata Gaps`, after the de Groat (2015) paragraph. Proposed text: "Bianchini and Munini (2025) show that infrastructure gaps also include the absence of practical classification systems for analog game collections. ClaG is promising because its facets map to use questions, but it still needs fuller treatment of traditional children's games, sports, mimicry and role-playing games, video games, large-scale field testing, and cross-institutional implementation."

(2022). Playing in limbo: Using liminal space to promote library services and writing center. *Journal of Play in Adulthood, 4*(1), 32-49. Unique identifier: `monnierPlayingLimboUsing2022`; Zotero item key `LLFJUUSX`; attachment key `ZFEYYAYD`. Monnier and Winters (2022) describe how Pittsburg State University's Axe Library and writing center used playful activities in liminal spaces to promote engagement with library services, writing support, and student well-being after a major renovation and the disruption of COVID-19. The article is grounded in a specific spatial problem. The writing center, student success office, coffee shop, tutoring programs, collaboration spaces, and library resources were located in or around the main library, but students tended to use only the service they intentionally came for. The authors therefore focused on thresholds and undefined areas between services: places neither fully classroom nor social space, neither only writing center nor only library. They argue that those spaces can become sites of unscripted, creative activity through play. The theoretical frame is liminality. Drawing on writing center studies and organizational space theory, the authors define liminal spaces as thresholds where different human worlds overlap and where new possibilities can emerge. Writing centers are already liminal because they combine instruction and service, safety and vulnerability, immediate help and long-term learning. Academic libraries are similarly ambiguous: they invite study, help-seeking, movement, collaboration, and social presence while also carrying expectations of quiet, seriousness, and individual academic work. This ambiguity can make students uncertain about how to engage. Monnier and Winters use play to lower that uncertainty and help students see the library as a campus home-away-from-home. The article describes several interventions. In the writing center, staff first tried a "build a story" activity on an easel pad, but patrons rarely participated, possibly because the activity required visible writing and risked judgment. Coloring pages became more successful because they were low-concentration, easy to start and stop, portable, and usable while waiting for appointments. A LEGO table gave patrons tactile materials; some used pieces as fidgets, while a few chemistry students built molecular models. Origami was less successful for general use because instructions were difficult for beginners, but it fit better in the writing center's international-student conversation program, where creative activity supported language practice. Staff also used card games, Connect 4, and other two-player games to sustain conversation partners' English practice. In the broader library, the authors describe passive and active play programming. Jigsaw puzzles near print queues and reading areas invited quick participation, reduced the awkwardness of waiting, and made the library feel like a place to work, relax, and spend time with friends. Coloring, glass-marker boards, oversized chess, checkers, International Games Week events, open gaming-group meetings, Dungeons & Dragons campaigns, a virtual escape room, virtual trivia, and Zoom game nights all helped test what kinds of play fit different spaces and pandemic conditions. The virtual escape room drew high engagement, with more than 700 clicks and more than 90 completed surveys. Their exploratory user feedback during International Games Week 2021 was modest but useful. Patrons interviewed while using playful activities repeatedly described them as fun, asked for more games, and moved portable games to other areas such as Axe Grind so larger groups could play. Writing center employees reported using playful activities with conversation partners at least weekly, sometimes multiple times per week. The authors observed that patrons gravitated toward spaces where playful activities were staged and that movable games helped users cross into liminal areas they might otherwise avoid. Their future plans were deliberately simple: add more multiplayer games, add games near Axe Grind, and add more two-player games and activities to the writing center. For the GBLS project, Monnier and Winters contribute an important space-and-facilitation perspective. Games do not have to be large, scripted programs to matter. Passive programming, portable materials, and visibly unfinished activities can reshape how patrons read an academic library's social permissions. Puzzles, coloring sheets, LEGO, cards, chess, checkers, and D&D can invite lingering, conversation, language practice, staff-patron contact, and service discovery. The article also reinforces that failed or partial experiments are informative: activities requiring too much cognitive effort, too much performance risk, or too many rules may not work well for patrons who are waiting, taking a break, or uncertain about whether play is acceptable in the space. The limitations are clear. The interviews were informal, time-limited, affected by the pandemic, and biased toward people already engaging in play. The article does not measure learning, service uptake, writing outcomes, or long-term belonging. Even so, it provides a practical GBLS lesson: start simple, observe patron behavior, adapt materials to local spaces, and let playful opportunities make ambiguous service boundaries easier to cross. Suggested review placement: Add to `# Major Areas of Game-Based Library Service` > `## Community, Belonging, and the Public Commons`, after the Giles et al. (2019) paragraph. Proposed text: "Monnier and Winters (2022) extend belonging from orientation events to everyday academic-library thresholds. At Pittsburg State University, puzzles, coloring pages, LEGO, card games, D&D, International Games Week activities, and writing- center conversation games helped students treat liminal areas between the library, writing center, coffee shop, and student services as acceptable places for relaxation, language practice, social interaction, and low-stakes service discovery." Suggested review placement: Add to `# Cross-Cutting Lessons for Game-Based Library Services` > `## Build Partnerships and Community Pipelines`, immediately after the heading. Proposed text: "Monnier and Winters (2022) show that GBLS partnerships can be spatial as well as organizational. Their library-writing center collaboration used small, portable, low-rule activities in threshold spaces to connect patrons with writing support, International Games Week, student organizations, conversation partners, D&D groups, and coffee-shop-adjacent gathering areas; the practical lesson is to start simple, observe where patrons move activities, and let play reveal where service boundaries feel inviting or uncertain."
